• Home
  • Tutorials
    • Transforming Variables
    • Making Comparisons
    • t-tests and ANOVAs
    • Chi-Square
    • Subsetting Data: Variable Types
    • Correlations
    • OLS: Linear Regressions
    • Logit: Logistic Regressions
    • Multivariate Regressions
    • Ordinal and Multinomial Logit
    • Publication-Ready Tables
  • Datasets
    • Spain Replication data
    • Test Scores data
  • Scripts
    • Capstone Script
  • Choosing the Appropriate Tests

Making Publication-Ready Tables

Author

Colin Hannigan

Agenda:

  1. Install Packages and Libraries
  2. The printC Option
  3. Publication-Ready Tables
    1. T-Tests
    2. ANOVAs
    3. Chi-square
    4. Linear Regression (OLS)
    5. Logistic Regression (Logit)
  4. Advanced Material
    1. Output stargazer() to .html file instead of text
    2. Correct SEs and p-values for Odds Ratios in stargazer()

1. Install Packages and Libraries:

New packages to install:

If you do not already have these packages, paste them manually into the console and install them.

#install.packages("stargazer")
#install.packages("oii")

  • stargazer allows us to produce clean, formatted regression tables.

  • oii provides additional measures of association for analyzing chi-square tests.

Load libraries!

library(RCPA3)
library(stargazer)
library(oii)

2. The printC Option

In RCPA3, printC() is its own standalone function that allows us to save information and output it in a html file, which means it’s formatted like a webpage. But many of the RCPA3 functions we already use also have a printC optional argument that we can use to ensure we have all the necessary components of the test.

You’ll notice that, whenever you use printC, you’ll get a message in the output that looks something like this: “…appended to Table.Output.Jun0326.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/Path/To/File.” The file name includes the current date, and the file will be updated (i.e., appended) to include the output from each printC statement you use in the same day. The message also includes the location of the file on your computer, so you can find it, open it in a browser and see the formatted text/visualizations.

3. Publication-Ready Tables for Each Test

T-tests

For a t-test, we can just add the printC = T optional argument at the end of the testmeansC() function:

testmeansC(states$vep20.turnout, states$vep16.turnout, paired = T, printC = T)
===========================================================================
                         Difference of Means Test
===========================================================================
Test Framework and Assumptions

Null Hypothesis: states$vep20.turnout - states$vep16.turnout = 0
Alt. Hypothesis: states$vep20.turnout - states$vep16.turnout != 0
Population standard deviation is unknown.
Observations are paired.
Statement appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs


Table: Descriptive Statistics

                         Mean       N   St. Dev.
---------------------  ------  ------  ---------
states$vep20.turnout    67.95   50.00       5.97
states$vep16.turnout    61.66   50.00       6.38
Table appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs



Table: t-Test Statistics

 Diff. of Means   SE of Diff.   t-Statistic      DF   P-value (2-tailed)
---------------  ------------  ------------  ------  -------------------
           6.29          0.36         17.31   49.00              1.6e-22
Table appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs



Table: 95% Confidence Interval of Difference of Means

 Lower Bound   Point Estimate   Upper Bound
------------  ---------------  ------------
        5.56             6.29          7.02
Table appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs
Image file added to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs

ANOVAs

We could use the same printC = T argument do something similar for an ANOVA if we take the compmeansC() route with the anova = T option to produce it. But because we need to do the additional Tukey HSD test to get all the necessary information for interpretation, we’ll use printC() as a standalone function instead.

  1. First, we’ll run the ANOVA using the aov() function and store the model as an object called anova.model1:
anova.model1 <- aov(world$gdp.percap~world$vdem.4cat)
  1. Next, we can use printC() to send the summary output of the model to the output document:
printC(summary(anova.model1))
Output appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs
  1. Then, we do the same thing with the Tukey HSD test output for the model:
printC(TukeyHSD(anova.model1))
Output appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs

Chi-square Crosstabs

The printC option is also helpful for crosstabs and chi-square tests:

  1. First, we run the model and store the output as an object (chitest1). Because we’re focused on getting the formatted output, we’ll be sure to include all the optional arguments in addition to the printC = T argument at the end:
chitest1<-crosstabC(dv=nes$gun.buying, iv=nes$libcon3, dvlabs = c("Harder", "Easier", "About the same"), compact=T, chisq = T, plot = F, printC = T)
===========================================================================
                         Cross-Tabulation Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Cross-Tabulation of nes$gun.buying and nes$libcon3

                    1. Liberal   2. Moderate   3. Conservative    Totals
-----------------  -----------  ------------  ----------------  --------
% Harder                 85.98         55.92             16.95     52.34
% Easier                  1.17          5.25             13.24      6.46
% About the same         12.85         38.83             69.81     41.20
Count                  1448.00       3183.00           1699.00   6330.00
Table appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     Chi-Squared Test of Independence
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Null hypothesis: nes$gun.buying and nes$libcon3 are independent. 


    Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  crosstab.observed.frequencies
X-squared = 1542.6, df = 4, p-value < 2.2e-16
Statement appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs
  1. Then, we can use the standalone printC() function to append the output of the association.measures() function on our stored model object to the file, too:
printC(association.measures(chitest1))
Output appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs

OLS Linear Regressions

For regression output, we can still use the printC = T argument as part of the regC() function, but it is always preferable to use stargazer() instead because it is more aligned with the formatting in the discipline and customizable.

But this is where things get a little tricky.

We could start by running the test using regC(), including the printC = T argument, and storing the model object as something like ols1. In the console, we’ll see that the model was appended to the output file as intended.

ols1 <- regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban, printC = T)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -9.433        1.241      -7.6      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.473      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.027        0.017     1.575      0.122
states$gini.2016                17.041        2.711     6.286      <.001
states$prcapinc                      0            0     2.274      0.028
states$urban                     0.001        0.004     0.231      0.818
Table appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.633    -0.222   -0.040     0.230   0.596
Table appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000
Statement appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs

But when we try using stargazer to print the model object to the console as text…

stargazer(ols1, type = "text")

% Error: Unrecognized object type.

…it doesn’t work!

That’s because regC() is a special function that is unique to the RCPA3 package.

So, we’ll create a new model object (ols2), copying the model equation into a new lm() function instead:

ols2 <- lm(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban)

As always, we can print the basic model summary to the console to have a look.

summary(ols2)

Call:
lm(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + 
    states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + 
    states$urban)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-0.63263 -0.22202 -0.04042  0.22996  0.59644 

Coefficients:
                             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                -9.433e+00  1.241e+00  -7.600 1.53e-09 ***
states$covid.cases.per1000  1.365e-02  2.109e-03   6.473 6.80e-08 ***
states$obesity.percent      2.660e-02  1.689e-02   1.575   0.1225    
states$gini.2016            1.704e+01  2.711e+00   6.286 1.28e-07 ***
states$prcapinc             3.017e-05  1.327e-05   2.274   0.0279 *  
states$urban                9.547e-04  4.132e-03   0.231   0.8183    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12

But we can also level up by using the stargazer() function to format the information in a table that we can copy/paste into a Word document.

Note: In Word, R output is best formatted using Lucida Console font (10pt size). If you don’t have that font option, though, you could try the Courier New font.

stargazer(ols2, type = "text")

===============================================
                        Dependent variable:    
                    ---------------------------
                       covid.deaths.per1000    
-----------------------------------------------
covid.cases.per1000          0.014***          
                              (0.002)          
                                               
obesity.percent                0.027           
                              (0.017)          
                                               
gini.2016                    17.041***         
                              (2.711)          
                                               
prcapinc                     0.00003**         
                             (0.00001)         
                                               
urban                          0.001           
                              (0.004)          
                                               
Constant                     -9.433***         
                              (1.241)          
                                               
-----------------------------------------------
Observations                    50             
R2                             0.754           
Adjusted R2                    0.726           
Residual Std. Error       0.320 (df = 44)      
F Statistic           26.938*** (df = 5; 44)   
===============================================
Note:               *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01

Next, let’s make a new model (ols3) that makes a slight adjustment to our original model. Like we’ve discussed before, we can compare related models to account for additional control variables, check for robustness across different measures of the same concept, include transformations, etc.

In this case, we’ll remove the states$obesity.percent independent variable from the model to see if the results change:

ols3 <- lm(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban)

Using stargazer(), we don’t have to look at two separate outputs to compare models; we can look at them side-by-side in the same output by just include both (or all) of them in the function:

stargazer(ols2, ols3, type = "text")

=================================================================
                                 Dependent variable:             
                    ---------------------------------------------
                                covid.deaths.per1000             
                             (1)                    (2)          
-----------------------------------------------------------------
covid.cases.per1000        0.014***               0.014***       
                           (0.002)                (0.002)        
                                                                 
obesity.percent             0.027                                
                           (0.017)                               
                                                                 
gini.2016                 17.041***              18.273***       
                           (2.711)                (2.638)        
                                                                 
prcapinc                  0.00003**               0.00002*       
                          (0.00001)              (0.00001)       
                                                                 
urban                       0.001                  -0.002        
                           (0.004)                (0.004)        
                                                                 
Constant                  -9.433***              -8.849***       
                           (1.241)                (1.204)        
                                                                 
-----------------------------------------------------------------
Observations                  50                     50          
R2                          0.754                  0.740         
Adjusted R2                 0.726                  0.717         
Residual Std. Error    0.320 (df = 44)        0.325 (df = 45)    
F Statistic         26.938*** (df = 5; 44) 32.000*** (df = 4; 45)
=================================================================
Note:                                 *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01

We can also, customize the stargazer output using stargazer’s optional arguments. To find these, you can either look at the documentation (linked at the top of this document) or take the easy route and look at the help information in the RStudio environment:

?stargazer
starting httpd help server ... done

For example, we might want to include the df = F argument to remove the degrees of freedom and save some horizontal spacing between columns:

stargazer(ols2, ols3, type = "text", df = F)

================================================
                        Dependent variable:     
                    ----------------------------
                        covid.deaths.per1000    
                          (1)           (2)     
------------------------------------------------
covid.cases.per1000    0.014***       0.014***  
                        (0.002)       (0.002)   
                                                
obesity.percent          0.027                  
                        (0.017)                 
                                                
gini.2016              17.041***     18.273***  
                        (2.711)       (2.638)   
                                                
prcapinc               0.00003**      0.00002*  
                       (0.00001)     (0.00001)  
                                                
urban                    0.001         -0.002   
                        (0.004)       (0.004)   
                                                
Constant               -9.433***     -8.849***  
                        (1.241)       (1.204)   
                                                
------------------------------------------------
Observations              50             50     
R2                       0.754         0.740    
Adjusted R2              0.726         0.717    
Residual Std. Error      0.320         0.325    
F Statistic            26.938***     32.000***  
================================================
Note:                *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01

Try some of these other options:

  • changing significance levels using star.cutoffs = c()
  • clarifying IV labels using covariate.labels = C()
  • making a DV label using dep.var.labels

Logistic Regressions (Logit)

The same logit applies to logistic regression output: printC() is possible, but stargazer() is preferable:

Just like before, we could start by running the test using logregC(), including the printC = T argument, and storing the model object as something like logit1. Even without the model object, the printC = T optional argument will append the tables to the output file.

logit1 <- logregC(formula = world$vdem.2cat ~ world$gdp.percap.5cat + world$frac.eth + world$pop.urban, fit.stats=T, printC = T)
===========================================================================
                       Logistic Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Logistic Regression Coefficients

                           Estimate   Std. Error   z stat   Pr(>|z|)
------------------------  ---------  -----------  -------  ---------
(Intercept)                   0.433        0.788     0.55      0.583
world$gdp.percap.5cat.L        2.13         0.75    2.841      0.004
world$gdp.percap.5cat.Q      -0.059        0.434   -0.137      0.891
world$gdp.percap.5cat.C       -0.75         0.44   -1.704      0.088
world$gdp.percap.5cat^4       0.342        0.381    0.897       0.37
world$frac.eth               -0.188        0.824   -0.228       0.82
world$pop.urban              -0.003        0.012   -0.266       0.79
Table appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs


Table: Deviance Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -1.735    -0.827    0.699     0.979   1.703
Table appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs


Additional Information:

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

Null deviance: 207.70  on 149  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 178.18  on 143  degrees of freedom
N: 150   (19 observations deleted due to missingness)
AIC: 192.18

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4
Dependent variable: world$vdem.2cat
Statement appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
                           Model Fit Statistics
---------------------------------------------------------------------------


Table: Model Fit Statistics

         Statistic    Value
------------------  -------
              Chi2   29.526
     DF (for Chi2)        6
   p-Value of Chi2    <.001
     McFadden's R2    0.142
 Cox & Snell Index    0.179
  Nagelkerke Index    0.239
Table appended to Table.Output.Jun2526.html in C:/Users/CHANNIGAN001/OneDrive - CSBSJU/POLS 222 Course Materials/Walkthroughs

Print the basic model summary to the console to have a look…

summary(logit1)

Call:
stats::glm(formula = formula, family = "binomial", data = data, 
    na.action = "na.omit")

Coefficients:
                         Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)              0.433164   0.788153   0.550  0.58260   
world$gdp.percap.5cat.L  2.130299   0.749737   2.841  0.00449 **
world$gdp.percap.5cat.Q -0.059252   0.433590  -0.137  0.89130   
world$gdp.percap.5cat.C -0.749643   0.439910  -1.704  0.08837 . 
world$gdp.percap.5cat^4  0.341534   0.380796   0.897  0.36977   
world$frac.eth          -0.187602   0.823607  -0.228  0.81982   
world$pop.urban         -0.003229   0.012148  -0.266  0.79038   
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 207.70  on 149  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 178.18  on 143  degrees of freedom
  (19 observations deleted due to missingness)
AIC: 192.18

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

…but once again, stargazer won’t work with an object created using the logregC() function:

stargazer(logit1, type = "text")

% Error: Unrecognized object type.

So, we’ll recreate the same model using the base R glm() function. This time we’ll assign it to an object called logit2. Remember: we always have to specify the family = "binomial" optional argument to skip over the default linear model.

logit2 <- glm(formula = world$vdem.2cat ~ world$gdp.percap.5cat + world$frac.eth + world$pop.urban, family = "binomial")

View the basic model summary in the console if you want…

summary(logit2)

Call:
glm(formula = world$vdem.2cat ~ world$gdp.percap.5cat + world$frac.eth + 
    world$pop.urban, family = "binomial")

Coefficients:
                         Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)              0.433164   0.788153   0.550  0.58260   
world$gdp.percap.5cat.L  2.130299   0.749737   2.841  0.00449 **
world$gdp.percap.5cat.Q -0.059252   0.433590  -0.137  0.89130   
world$gdp.percap.5cat.C -0.749643   0.439910  -1.704  0.08837 . 
world$gdp.percap.5cat^4  0.341534   0.380796   0.897  0.36977   
world$frac.eth          -0.187602   0.823607  -0.228  0.81982   
world$pop.urban         -0.003229   0.012148  -0.266  0.79038   
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 207.70  on 149  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 178.18  on 143  degrees of freedom
  (19 observations deleted due to missingness)
AIC: 192.18

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

…then put it into a stargazer() function. The function below is basic, but you can add any useful modifications, just like we did above with the OLS linear regression models.

stargazer(logit2, type = "text")

=============================================
                      Dependent variable:    
                  ---------------------------
                           vdem.2cat         
---------------------------------------------
gdp.percap.5cat.L          2.130***          
                            (0.750)          
                                             
gdp.percap.5cat.Q           -0.059           
                            (0.434)          
                                             
gdp.percap.5cat.C           -0.750*          
                            (0.440)          
                                             
gdp.percap.5cat4             0.342           
                            (0.381)          
                                             
frac.eth                    -0.188           
                            (0.824)          
                                             
pop.urban                   -0.003           
                            (0.012)          
                                             
Constant                     0.433           
                            (0.788)          
                                             
---------------------------------------------
Observations                  150            
Log Likelihood              -89.089          
Akaike Inf. Crit.           192.178          
=============================================
Note:             *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01

Notice that stargazer reports the raw log-odds rather than the odds ratios. While we might be tempted to try apply.coef = exp to exponentiate the coefficients and present odds ratios, that’s a dangerous change because it also biases the standard errors, test statistics, and p-values.

So, to report odds ratios and classification tables, it’s reasonable to pull those from the printC = T output of the logregC() version of the model(s), including the fit.stats = T, orci = T, and pre = T options. Then just screenshot and copy/paste those tables separately.

4. Advanced Material:

Output stargazer() to .html file instead of text

Formatting the stargazer() table as an html document makes it look even better, but it can be sort of complicated. The key here is to change the type = from "text" to "html" and provide a memorable file name in the out = argument for where the output is saved.

stargazer(logit2, type = "html", out = "logit2ResultsTable.html")

<table style="text-align:center"><tr><td colspan="2" style="border-bottom: 1px solid black"></td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td><em>Dependent variable:</em></td></tr>
<tr><td></td><td colspan="1" style="border-bottom: 1px solid black"></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td>vdem.2cat</td></tr>
<tr><td colspan="2" style="border-bottom: 1px solid black"></td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left">gdp.percap.5cat.L</td><td>2.130<sup>***</sup></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td>(0.750)</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left">gdp.percap.5cat.Q</td><td>-0.059</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td>(0.434)</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left">gdp.percap.5cat.C</td><td>-0.750<sup>*</sup></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td>(0.440)</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left">gdp.percap.5cat4</td><td>0.342</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td>(0.381)</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left">frac.eth</td><td>-0.188</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td>(0.824)</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left">pop.urban</td><td>-0.003</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td>(0.012)</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left">Constant</td><td>0.433</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td>(0.788)</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left"></td><td></td></tr>
<tr><td colspan="2" style="border-bottom: 1px solid black"></td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left">Observations</td><td>150</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left">Log Likelihood</td><td>-89.089</td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:left">Akaike Inf. Crit.</td><td>192.178</td></tr>
<tr><td colspan="2" style="border-bottom: 1px solid black"></td></tr><tr><td style="text-align:left"><em>Note:</em></td><td style="text-align:right"><sup>*</sup>p<0.1; <sup>**</sup>p<0.05; <sup>***</sup>p<0.01</td></tr>
</table>

You’ll see the html code printed to the console, too, but you can find the html file that you produced and open it in a browser, then select all and copy/paste it into your Word document. Remember: you can always add in other optional arguments, just like before, to maximize the look/clarity of your table.

Correct SEs and p-values for Odds Ratios in stargazer()

  1. First, we need to calculate the odds ratios and store them in a separate object:
OddsRatios <- exp(coef(logit2))

  1. Then, we correct the standard errors and store those in a separate object, too.

    Because odds ratios are transformed versions of the original coefficients (log-odds), the standard errors change, too. So, we go back to the original model (specifically, something called a variance-covariance matrix, which is a table of the uncertainty of the estimates) to recalculate the standard errors in light of the odds ratio transformations. To do this we multiply the odds ratios my the square root of the diagonal (the Maximum Likelihood Estimates) from the variance-covariance matrix of the original model:

SE_Odds <- OddsRatios * sqrt(diag(vcov(logit2)))
  1. Then, we can pass arguments to stargazer to use our new OddsRatios object in place of the coefficients, and our new SE_Odds object in place of the original standard errors. Then we turn off the automatic t-statistics and p-values that stargazer() defaults to because the original ones were wrong. Basically: “just use my inputs rather than ignoring them in favor of the original model.”
stargazer(logit2, type = "text",
          coef = list(OddsRatios), 
          se = list(SE_Odds), 
          t.auto = FALSE, 
          p.auto = FALSE)

=============================================
                      Dependent variable:    
                  ---------------------------
                           vdem.2cat         
---------------------------------------------
gdp.percap.5cat.L          8.417***          
                            (6.311)          
                                             
gdp.percap.5cat.Q            0.942           
                            (0.409)          
                                             
gdp.percap.5cat.C           0.473*           
                            (0.208)          
                                             
gdp.percap.5cat4             1.407           
                            (0.536)          
                                             
frac.eth                     0.829           
                            (0.683)          
                                             
pop.urban                    0.997           
                            (0.012)          
                                             
Constant                     1.542           
                            (1.215)          
                                             
---------------------------------------------
Observations                  150            
Log Likelihood              -89.089          
Akaike Inf. Crit.           192.178          
=============================================
Note:             *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01
